title: Testing Generative AI Systems
concept:
Testing Generative AI systems (LLMs, image generators, multimodal models) presents unique challenges that require adapted testing approaches.

**Black-box functional testing**: Test with diverse inputs — varied prompts, partial information, multilingual inputs, edge-case queries, sensitive topics. Assess output quality against qualitative criteria: accuracy, clarity, relevance, safety, coherence, and appropriate refusals.

**Automated evaluation**: A second AI system can automatically evaluate GenAI outputs. For example, an image recognition model can verify that a generated image actually contains the requested object. LLMs can score other LLM responses for factual accuracy or policy compliance (LLM-as-judge). This addresses scalability — human review of millions of outputs is impractical.

**Non-functional testing**: GenAI models are compute-intensive. Key non-functional tests include:
- Resource utilisation (CPU/GPU usage, memory footprint)
- Latency (time-to-first-token, total generation time)
- Throughput (requests per second)
- Cost per inference

**Benchmark suites**: Standardised evaluation datasets (MMLU, HellaSwag, TruthfulQA) provide reproducible comparisons between model versions and between competing models.

**Key challenges unique to GenAI:**
- **Input explosion**: Natural language has a near-infinite input space; systematic coverage is impossible.
- **Context window effects**: Long conversations may behave differently from short ones.
- **Temperature sensitivity**: Outputs vary significantly with temperature setting — tests should specify temperature.
keyPoints: GenAI testing uses diverse black-box inputs assessed against qualitative criteria (accuracy, clarity, safety) — a second AI can automatically evaluate outputs at scale.
Non-functional testing (latency, GPU utilisation, throughput) is critical for GenAI systems due to their high compute requirements.
The input explosion problem — the near-infinite space of natural language prompts — makes exhaustive functional coverage impossible; risk-based prioritisation is essential.

quiz.question: A team is testing a text-to-image AI model and needs to verify that generated images actually contain the objects described in prompts at scale (millions of images). Which approach is most practical?
options: Human review of all generated images by trained testers.
Use an image recognition AI model to automatically classify generated images and verify they contain the requested objects.
Set temperature to 0 and assume all outputs are identical, reducing the review to a single sample.
Only test prompts that match the training data distribution to ensure deterministic outputs.
quiz.explanation: At scale, human review is impractical. Using an image recognition AI as an automated evaluator (LLM-as-judge pattern) enables scalable, consistent evaluation of generated content. Setting temperature to 0 does not guarantee identical outputs for different prompts, and testing only training-distribution prompts misses out-of-distribution failures.
